THE OMNIMETA SAGA: CANONICAL BLUEPRINT
A Complete Reference Bible for the Confluence Chronicles
A comprehensive guide to the thirty-novella epic spanning the world of Te’Oga, from the Piercing through divine awakening to universal consequence
----
PART I: THE WORLD OF TE’OGA
THE PIERCING
On Tide 0 of what would become known as the First Turning, an object of unprecedented scale penetrated Te’Oga’s atmosphere. Not a meteor—the trajectory was wrong, the entry angle impossible by natural physics. The Piercing, as it came to be called, was a cylindrical mass roughly three hundred paces in diameter and extending beyond visual range, moving at speeds that bent light around its passage.
The object never struck the surface. Instead, it passed through the planet’s upper atmosphere, raking across the sky like a finger drawn through water, and continued into deep space beyond. But its passage delivered catastrophic energy into Te’Oga’s planetary systems:
Immediate Effects:
•  Atmospheric compression waves that shattered structures across half the continent
•  Seismic activity triggering widespread building collapse in urban centers
•  Introduction of alien particulate matter into the atmosphere (fine metallic dust, faintly luminescent, composition unknown)
•  Disruption of traditional physics at the quantum level, enabling what would become known as Soulpulse
Long-term Consequences:
•  Weakening of ancient seals containing four imprisoned cosmic entities
•  Gradual emergence of Soulpulse abilities in population (approximately 1 in 500 people show capacity)
•  Structural instability across all major cities requiring constant maintenance
•  Atmospheric changes creating persistent haze and altered light quality
•  Social upheaval as traditional authority structures fail during crisis
The Piercing wasn’t random. It was a weapon, deliberately deployed, though by whom and for what purpose remains unknown to the people of Te’Oga. What they do know is that their world fundamentally changed in the span of heartbeats, and nothing will ever return to what it was before.
SOULPULSE: THE COST OF REACHING
In the immediate aftermath of the Piercing, certain individuals discovered they could affect physical reality through focused intention. This wasn’t magic in any traditional sense—no incantations, no reagents, no mystical gestures. Instead, it emerged from desperate need: a parent trying to hold a collapsing ceiling long enough for children to escape, a healer trying to mend a wound that wouldn’t close, a builder trying to stabilize a structure on the verge of total failure.
The common element was always protective intention crystallized through physical contact.
How Soulpulse Activates:
•  Requires skin contact with target material (stone, metal, flesh, wood)
•  Demands emotional investment—specifically, desperate need to protect or preserve
•  Doesn’t work through casual desire or intellectual will alone
•  The user must need the material to hold/mend/stabilize
•  Activation manifests as copper-gold warmth spreading from palms into target
Physical Manifestation:
•  Visual: Copper-gold tracery appears on the user’s forearms, following vein patterns but extending beyond normal anatomy
•  Tactile: Intense pressure in palms, vibration that extends from hands up through arms into chest
•  Sensory: The user feels connected to the material they’re affecting, sensing its stress points and structural integrity
•  Observable: Others can see the vein-lines when they’re active, watch the glow spread from contact points
The Cost System:
Soulpulse draws from the user’s physical reserves, but not in the way normal exertion does. It’s more fundamental—a drawing down of the body’s baseline coherence. Every use leaves residue.
Stage 1 (Minor Tremor):
•  Appears after first 2-3 Soulpulse uses
•  Slight tremor in hands, noticeable when trying to perform fine motor tasks
•  Fades after several spans of rest
•  User can still function normally
•  Most people don’t progress beyond this stage if they use Soulpulse sparingly
Stage 2 (Baseline Tremor):
•  Develops after 5-10 uses within short timeframe (single Tide)
•  Tremor becomes constant baseline, doesn’t fully fade with rest
•  Vein-lines remain faintly visible even when not actively using power
•  Deep exhaustion that sleep doesn’t fully resolve
•  Fine motor control compromised
•  Users can still work but efficiency drops
•  This becomes the new normal—body has been fundamentally changed
Stage 3 (Debilitating):
•  Occurs after sustained heavy use (10+ uses in single Tide, or dual-point holds)
•  Tremor intensifies to where hands are nearly useless
•  Vein-lines permanently visible, pulse with copper-gold even at rest
•  Chronic pain in palms and forearms
•  Difficulty walking (tremor extends to legs)
•  Cognitive fog from constant low-level pain
•  Sleep becomes difficult
•  Users at this stage can barely function in daily life
•  Reversible with extended rest (multiple Anneals) but leaves permanent sensitivity
Stage 4 (Potentially Fatal):
•  Theoretical stage discussed but not yet reached in N01-N05
•  Tremor extends to full body
•  Vein-lines spreading beyond arms into chest and neck
•  Risk of permanent neurological damage
•  Possibility of cascade failure where body forgets how to maintain basic functions
•  May be irreversible
Special Cost Factors:
Dual-Point Holding: When a user attempts to affect two separate points simultaneously (like Jhace holding both a column and a beam), the cost doesn’t double—it increases exponentially. Holding two points costs roughly four times what holding one point costs.
Sustained Duration: The longer a hold is maintained, the faster cost accumulates. A ten-second hold might cost X. A span-long hold might cost 10X or more.
Frequency: Using Soulpulse repeatedly without rest compounds cost. First use in a Tide: minimal cost. Fifth use in same Tide: significantly higher cost even for same task.
Emotional State: Paradoxically, the more desperate the need, the easier activation becomes—but the less the user notices accumulating cost until it’s severe.
THE DIVINE PRINCIPLES
Unknown to most mortals, four fundamental cosmic forces govern reality on Te’Oga. These aren’t abstract philosophical concepts—they’re active entities, imprisoned in mortal form by an event from deep history that no living person remembers.
The Four Principles:
FORM (Xilcore):
Order, structure, geometric perfection, systematic arrangement, the belief that reality functions best when organized according to precise rules. Form creates frameworks, establishes hierarchies, builds systems that self-perpetuate. It abhors chaos, resists change, seeks to crystallize all things into their ideal static arrangement.
WHOLENESS (Leesa):
Connection, mending, empathy networks, the belief that all things are fundamentally unified and that separation creates suffering. Wholeness seeks to bridge divides, repair ruptures, maintain bonds between entities. It abhors isolation, resists fragmentation, works to keep all things in relationship.
PURITY (Blemo):
Refinement, distillation, essence extraction, the belief that contamination must be removed to reveal truth. Purity seeks to separate clean from unclean, worthy from unworthy, essential from extraneous. It abhors mixture, resists compromise, works to isolate singular perfect elements.
ENTROPY (Seeri):
Dissolution, release, necessary decay, the belief that clinging to forms creates suffering and that letting go is liberation. Entropy seeks to break down structures, free trapped energy, return complex systems to simpler states. It abhors preservation, resists stasis, works to ensure all things eventually dissolve.
The Fundamental Opposition:
These four Principles don’t just disagree philosophically—they oppose each other at the level of cosmic law. Form and Entropy are natural enemies (structure vs. dissolution). Wholeness and Purity are natural enemies (connection vs. separation). But the opposition is more complex:
•  Form vs. Wholeness: Rigid systems vs. flexible connections
•  Form vs. Purity: Systematic order vs. distilled essence
•  Wholeness vs. Entropy: Maintaining bonds vs. releasing them
•  Purity vs. Entropy: Isolated perfection vs. dispersed dissolution
Every Principle opposes every other Principle. Alliance is cosmically impossible. When these forces were active and aware, they generated constant conflict—a grinding divine war that threatened to tear reality apart through sheer contradiction.
Someone, at some point in forgotten history, sealed them. Imprisoned them in mortal forms with suppressed memories, hoping to end the eternal war by making the gods forget they were gods.
It worked. For Casts.
Until the Piercing began weakening the seals.
GUILD STRUCTURE AND CITY GEOGRAPHY
Saint Merro is the primary city where our story begins—a major urban center in Te’Oga’s central continent, built in terraced layers up a hillside basin with the river at the base and the Crown district at the peak.
District Structure (Low to High):
The Dregs (Lowest):
•  River-level slums and industrial zones
•  Oldest construction, poorest maintenance
•  Most vulnerable to structural failure
•  Dense population, limited resources
•  Where most refugees gather post-Piercing
•  Home to Eries’s growing cult following
Underface Districts:
•  Middle-tier residential and commercial zones
•  Built partially into hillside, partially above ground
•  Where Jhace’s forge and most guild operations exist
•  Heat-grid maintenance tunnels run beneath these areas
•  Mixed quality construction—some good, some failing
Glass Quarter:
•  Upper-middle district specializing in precision crafts
•  Where Tiffani works in the precision glass shop
•  Observatory tower at highest point of this district
•  Better construction but still vulnerable
•  Mix of workshops and wealthier residential
The Crown:
•  Highest district, institutional and political center
•  Best construction, most resources
•  Where council meetings and governance occur
•  Where Corlexi rises through bureaucratic ranks
•  Least affected by Piercing damage initially
Guild System:
Guilds control craft knowledge, training, and trade licensing. Major guilds include:
•  Forgemasters Guild: Metalworking, structural repair, heat management (where Jhace and Bram work)
•  Glassworkers Guild: Precision crafts, optics, observation instruments (where Tiffani works)
•  Builders Guild: Architecture, construction, large-scale projects
•  Thermal Guild: Heat-grid maintenance, energy distribution
•  Healers Collective: Medical care, anatomy knowledge (not technically a guild, more decentralized)
Guilds are protective of their knowledge, suspicious of innovation that threatens their control. The emergence of Soulpulse terrifies them because it enables people to affect structures without formal training—which undermines guild authority.
Post-Piercing, guilds are fracturing. Some advocate strict regulation of Soulpulse users. Others want to study and integrate the ability. Still others deny it exists. This conflict drives much of the political tension in early novellas.
TIME AND MEASUREMENT SYSTEMS
Time Units:
•  Tide: Approximately 24 hours (one full day-night cycle)
•  Anneal: 5 Tides (roughly a week)
•  Crucible: 35 Tides (roughly a month)
•  Turn: Personal age measurement (e.g., “29 Turns old” = 29 years old)
•  Cast: Historical time (generations, eras—imprecise)
Time Phases Within a Tide:
•  Kindling: Dawn, early morning
•  Pouring: Mid-morning to noon
•  Shaping: Afternoon
•  Striking: Late afternoon, early evening
•  Quench: Dusk, night
Distance:
•  Span: Arm’s length (roughly 3 feet)
•  Pace: Single stride (roughly 3 feet)
•  Pole: Standard measuring rod (roughly 10 feet)
Volume:
•  Thimble: Small amount
•  Cup: Standard drinking vessel
•  Cask: Large storage container
Weight:
•  Grain: Tiny amount
•  Stone: Heavy object (roughly 10-15 pounds)
•  Weight: Formal measurement unit
Directional Language:
No cardinal directions (north/south/east/west). Instead:
•  “Toward the river” (downslope)
•  “Up-slope” (toward Crown)
•  “Harbor-side” (toward port)
•  “Inland” (away from water)
TECHNOLOGY LEVEL
Pre-Piercing Baseline:
•  Renaissance/proto-industrial equivalent
•  Water mills, windmills for power
•  Simple steam engines (boilers, pumps)
•  Printing presses exist but are expensive
•  Basic gunpowder exists but rare (not militarily common)
•  No electricity, no telegraph, no rail
•  Lighting: oil lamps, candles
•  Communication: runners, courier networks, signal flags from towers
•  Medicine: herbal, surgical (no germ theory but practical anatomy knowledge)
•  Metallurgy: advanced (can produce quality steel, alloys)
Post-Piercing Changes:
•  Infrastructure failing faster than it can be repaired
•  Traditional engineering increasingly inadequate
•  Soulpulse emerging as supplement/replacement for mechanical systems
•  Social structures breaking down as guilds lose authority
•  Technology advancement stalled while society focuses on survival
----
PART II: THE MORTAL PROTAGONISTS
JHACE TORRINS: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS
Age: 29 Turns
Profession: Structural engineer, heat-grid inspector, forgemaster (unaffiliated with guild leadership)
Physical Description: Wiry build from constant movement rather than bulk. Weathered hands, permanent tension in shoulders. Dark hair pulled back in short tail. Canvas work clothes, heavy leather tool belt. Moves with efficient precision—no wasted motion.
Core Trauma:
When Jhace was 7 Turns old, his parents died in a residential building collapse. Twenty-three people killed total. The investigation concluded “material fatigue”—support beam failed, structure came down. No one caught the problem in time. No one was vigilant enough.
Jhace has spent the last 22 Turns trying to be vigilant enough.
Psychology:
Obsessive Prevention Through Vigilance: Jhace doesn’t just inspect structures—he catalogs every potential failure point. Walking through city, he’s constantly assessing: that wall has a crack, that beam is loaded wrong, that joint will fail under stress. He sees the thousand ways buildings can kill people, and he can’t unsee it.
Isolation as Armor: He works alone because teaching someone else to see what he sees means teaching them to carry the same burden. “That’s not a skill, that’s anxiety that happens to be useful.” He refuses to train apprentices because he believes he’d be inflicting his particular form of hypervigilance on them.
The Knot: The psychological trap he’s caught in: “There are thousands of people in this city. I will spend the rest of my life unable to save most of them. I will feel responsible for all of them. This is what it means to have opened my eyes to what I can do.” Every person he saves reminds him of the ones he couldn’t reach. Every successful repair reminds him of the failures happening elsewhere.
What He Fears:
Failing to prevent a death he could have prevented. Not death itself—preventable death. The idea that someone died because he wasn’t vigilant enough, wasn’t there in time, didn’t check the right structure. This fear drives everything.
What He Regrets:
Every person he’s walked past while responding to a crisis. Every time he had to choose who to help because he couldn’t help everyone. The specific people whose names he knows who died while he was saving someone else. The list is long and getting longer.
What He Lies About:
“I’m fine.” He’s not fine. Hasn’t been since age 7. The obsessive vigilance is exhausting. The guilt is crushing. The inability to stop trying is destroying him slowly. But if he admits he’s not fine, people might try to make him stop, and stopping means people die. So: “I’m fine.”
What He Pretends to Know:
How to connect with people normally. He’s been so focused on preventing deaths that he’s forgotten how to just be with someone without cataloging the ways their environment might kill them. He pretends he’s chosen isolation because it’s practical. Reality: he doesn’t know how to be close to people anymore and is terrified to try.
His “Humor”:
Jhace thinks he’s funny. He is not funny.
His jokes are dry, deadpan observations delivered with zero awareness that they’re not landing. Example: Standing in rubble after building collapse, someone asks “Could this get worse?” Jhace responds: “The building could catch fire. Or the adjacent structure could fail and crush us. Or—” Complete sincerity. Doesn’t realize the person wanted reassurance, not technical assessment.
Embarrassing Hiccups (Running Character Traits):
1.  Physical Awareness Gap: Brilliant at assessing structural stability, completely oblivious to his own body in social space. Constantly bumping into people, backing into things, standing too close or too far. When Tiffani first tries to position herself near him, he moves away unconsciously—not rejection, just spatial unawareness.
2.  Failed Compliments: Tries to say something kind, comes out weird. To Tiffani after she saves situation with brilliant analysis: “Your brain works good.” Immediate cringe from everyone. He meant it genuinely.
3.  Literalism: Cannot detect sarcasm. When someone says “Great, another crisis, exactly what we needed,” Jhace responds seriously: “We didn’t need this. This is bad actually.” Tiffani finds this endearing; others find it exhausting.
4.  The Snort: When he’s genuinely amused (rare), he snort-laughs before he can stop himself. Hates this about himself. Tiffani loves it, tries to make him snort-laugh as often as possible.
5.  Standing Weird: When thinking hard, stands in bizarre poses—balanced on one foot, tilted at odd angle, hand on hip like he’s perpetually about to sit down but never does. Completely unaware he’s doing it. Bram has learned to just let it happen.
His Actual Laugh:
When something is genuinely funny to Jhace (which is rare), he cackles. Not chuckles—CACKLES. Loud, unrestrained, completely unselfconscious. It’s the only time he’s not performing control. The sound is sharp, almost alarming if you’re not expecting it, and it echoes.
Tiffani discovers she loves making him cackle. It becomes her secret mission: find what genuinely amuses him (not what he thinks should be funny) and deploy it strategically.
Voice Pattern:
Jhace thinks in physical sensations and structural metaphors. His internal monologue is sparse, body-focused:
“Hands know before mind does. Copper-gold warmth spreading. The beam needs to hold. Make it hold.”
His speech is terse, functional, defensive:
“The wall’s failing.”
“How do you know?”
“I know.”
He doesn’t explain his intuition because he doesn’t fully understand it himself. He just feels wrongness in structures the way other people feel when someone’s watching them.
When exhausted or overwhelmed, his speech gets even more clipped:
“Can’t.”
“Can’t what?”
“Just. Can’t.”
But occasionally, when truly safe (alone with Tiffani, late at night, too tired to maintain walls), he opens up. His sentences get longer. He explains what he sees. These moments are rare and precious.
Relationship with Tiffani:
Starts with mutual judgment. He thinks she’s a perfectionist who prevents work from getting done. She thinks he’s overextending out of ego. Both are completely wrong about each other.
Actual dynamic: They’re the same person using different methods. He prevents deaths through hypervigilant physical intervention. She prevents deaths through careful measurement and prediction. His body-knowledge + her systematic analysis = actually complementary.
The attraction is competence recognition first, then emotional recognition (oh, you’re as broken as I am), then physical (complicated by exhaustion and crisis), then genuine partnership.
Soulpulse Development (N01-N05):
•  N01: First instinctive activation saving Chloen, second holding building. No understanding of what he’s doing. Stage 1 tremor develops.
•  N02: Learning to activate deliberately with emotional investment. Discovers cost increases with use. Stage 2 tremor becomes baseline. Begins working with Tiffani to optimize technique.
•  N03: First dual-point hold. Discovers cost is exponential, not linear. Reaches Stage 3 briefly, pulls back with rest. Recognizes he’s pushing too hard but can’t stop.
•  N04: Sustained holds over longer durations. Body adapting but also breaking down. Vein-lines becoming semi-permanent. Tiffani actively managing his recovery to prevent Stage 4.
•  N05: The limit. Everything he’s learned, everything he can do, pushed to maximum during crisis leading to Tiffani’s death. The Fathom Break: grief-powered eruption that shatters limits and breaks ancient seals. Survives but fundamentally changed.
----
TIFFANI MERROW: THE PATTERN READER
Age: 27 Turns
Profession: Precision glassworker, atmospheric observer, structural analyst
Physical Description: Lean (forgets to eat when focused). Dark brown hair with sun-streaks, pulled into practical knot. Canvas apron over simple clothes. Hands scarred from glass-work—thin white lines on palms and fingers. Moves with deliberate efficiency, except when leg is injured (post-Piercing splinted leg she refuses to rest properly).
Core Trauma:
Marisse died because Tiffani missed a pattern.
They were working together in a glasshouse, annealing fresh pieces in the cooling oven. Tiffani was monitoring temperature fluctuations—small variances she dismissed as “normal operational noise.” Thermal stress in the oven walls. Nothing dramatic. Just… fluctuations.
Until the glass shattered during cooldown. Large piece. High velocity. Through Marisse’s throat.
Twenty-three years old. Dead before Tiffani could reach her.
The investigation: “Material fatigue in glass, stress fracture from uneven cooling.” But Tiffani knew—she’d seen the thermal pattern. She’d noted it. She’d dismissed it as irrelevant.
She’d been wrong.
Psychology:
Pattern Recognition as Atonement: If Tiffani can see patterns early enough, she can warn people before disaster becomes inevitable. Every measurement is saying: “I’m paying attention now. I won’t miss it again.” She’s not trying to be perfect—she’s trying to notice the small wrong things before they become large deadly things.
Precision Disguises Care: People think she’s rigid and perfectionist. She’s not. She’s terrified and meticulous. The precision isn’t about perfection—it’s about saving lives through early warning. She turns down commissions not because they’re beneath her standards, but because accepting inadequate specifications means people could die when the structure fails. She’d rather be called impossible than be responsible for another death.
Connection-Oriented: Despite reputation as cold analyst, Tiffani is fundamentally driven by desire to connect and mend. She wants to understand systems so she can explain them to others so everyone can be safer together. Her analytical approach is service-oriented: “If I understand this, I can teach it, and then we’re all less vulnerable.”
What She Fears:
Missing another pattern. Specifically: noticing something is wrong, dismissing it as unimportant, and then watching someone die because of her dismissal. The fear isn’t “I’ll fail to notice”—it’s “I’ll notice and decide it doesn’t matter, and be wrong.” Trusting her own judgment terrifies her.
What She Regrets:
That she didn’t check the oven more carefully. That she prioritized schedule over safety. That she told Marisse “it’s fine” when Marisse asked if the temperature variance was concerning. That she was annoyed by Marisse’s concern instead of investigating. The specific dismissive tone she used when saying “it’s fine.”
What She Lies About:
That she’s confident in her assessments. She’s not. Every analysis she delivers sounds certain because sounding uncertain makes people panic or ignore warnings. But internally, she’s constantly questioning: Am I missing something? Is this pattern real or am I seeing ghosts? If I’m wrong, who dies?
What She Pretends to Know:
How to rest. How to stop working when the problem isn’t solved yet. How to accept that some things are beyond her control. Everyone assumes her relentless observation habits are personality—actually, she just doesn’t know how to turn off the vigilance without feeling like she’s abandoning her responsibility. She pretends she chooses to work this much. Reality: she doesn’t know how to stop.
Her ACTUAL Humor:
Tiffani is funny when she tries to be funny. Unlike Jhace (who thinks he’s funny but isn’t), she can read social situations well enough to know what will land.
Her humor is:
•  Sharp observational wit - seeing absurdity others miss
•  Self-deprecating - usually about her own social awkwardness
•  Strategically deployed sex jokes - near-perverted, saved for specific moments
The Sex Jokes:
Tiffani’s signature humor is sex jokes that are almost too much but perfectly timed. She only uses them when:
1.  She’s genuinely attracted/aroused - it’s how she flirts
2.  She wants to make Jhace laugh specifically - loves his cackle-laugh
3.  Tension needs breaking - dark moment needs puncture
Examples of her style:
[After Jhace completes difficult Soulpulse hold, is visibly exhausted, hands shaking]
Tiffani, casual: “If your hands shake like that during other activities, I have concerns.”
Jhace: [snort-laugh that becomes cackle]
[Examining structural joint that requires specific angle]
Tiffani: “Getting the angle right matters more than force. Same principle everywhere.”
Jhace: [completely misses innuendo]
Bram: [gets it immediately, groans]
[Late night, both exhausted, Jhace mentions being too tired to think]
Tiffani: “Thinking is overrated. I can list several better activities that don’t require cognition.”
Jhace: [takes full span to process, then cackles]
She’s not crude—she’s precise with her crudeness. The jokes are surgical strikes. Calibrated to audience. She’d never make them in front of most people. But with Jhace (and sometimes Bram), once she’s comfortable? She’s fearless.
Other Humor Notes:
•  Enjoys making Jhace laugh because his cackle is joyful and unguarded
•  Sometimes engineers situations specifically to provoke his snort-laugh
•  Will absolutely make serious technical observation with completely straight face, watch Jhace deliver terrible joke in response, then cackle at his attempt (not the joke itself—his earnest delivery)
•  Has running commentary about Jhace’s weird standing positions: “Is that load-bearing? Are you testing structural integrity with your hip?”
Voice Pattern:
Tiffani thinks in measurements and pattern recognition:
“Three subdivisions off center. Not possible through normal atmospheric refraction. Pattern indicates… what? Need more data. But if the pattern holds, then divergence will compound by factor of… estimation: six. No, eight. Need to verify.”
Her speech is precise, analytical, but warmer than people expect:
“The beam is the wrong target. If we stabilize here and here, we can shift the load path around it.”
She explains her reasoning not to show off, but because she wants people to understand so they can help. Her technical language has underlying care that becomes obvious once you’re listening for it.
When exhausted or stressed, she gets more talkative (opposite of Jhace):
“I’m calculating the stress vectors but the angles aren’t quite right and if I’m wrong about the fulcrum point then we’re wasting time and she’s dying and I can’t afford to be wrong again but I don’t have instruments and I’m estimating and—”
Jhace has learned to gently interrupt these spirals with: “What’s your best guess?” She needs permission to be uncertain.
Mistakes She Makes (N01-N05):
Mistake 1 (N02): Miscalculates structural load during rescue operation. Assumes beam will hold because math says it should. Doesn’t account for heat damage weakening the structure beyond her mathematical model. Beam partially fails. No one dies, but someone is injured worse than they would’ve been. Tiffani is devastated. Jhace tells her: “You used best information you had. I held the beam wrong anyway. We both contributed to that. We learn.”
Mistake 2 (N03): Overestimates her ability to predict human behavior patterns the way she predicts structural patterns. Tries to use measurement methodology to assess who’s trustworthy in refugee group. Identifies someone as “statistically safe” based on behavioral patterns. They steal medical supplies. Multiple people suffer because medicine is gone. Tiffani realizes: people aren’t structures. Patterns are messier. She’s applied wrong framework to wrong problem.
Mistake 3 (N04): Pushes Jhace too hard trying to optimize his Soulpulse technique. She’s so focused on “reducing wasted effort” that she doesn’t notice he’s approaching Stage 3. Her analysis is correct but her application is dangerous. Jhace nearly collapses during critical rescue. He survives but it’s close. Tiffani has to confront: her drive to understand can become its own kind of recklessness. She promises to track his limits, not just his technique.
What She Learns:
•  Math isn’t enough—need to account for unmeasured variables
•  People ≠ structures (different analytical framework needed)
•  Understanding systems doesn’t mean you can push them to optimize
•  Sometimes “good enough” is better than “perfect but late”
•  Her mistakes aren’t Marisse’s death repeated—they’re different mistakes, and she can learn from them without being destroyed by them
Relationship with Jhace:
Mutual recognition of kindred broken person. He prevents deaths through physical intervention. She prevents deaths through measurement and prediction. Both are trying to atone for failures they blame themselves for.
The partnership is:
•  Complementary Skills: His intuition + her analysis = better outcomes than either alone
•  Mutual Understanding: Both know what it’s like to feel responsible for everyone
•  Different Breaking Points: He shuts down when overwhelmed; she spirals verbally. They can catch each other.
•  Attraction Through Competence: She finds his commitment attractive. He finds her precision (that’s actually care) attractive.
•  Physical Connection: Complicated by exhaustion, crisis, injury. But the desire is there, building, occasionally expressed through her sex jokes and his rare open moments.
Her Role in His Soulpulse Development:
She can’t use Soulpulse herself. But she can analyze it. Her contribution:
•  Identifying that cost is exponential not linear
•  Recognizing emotional state correlation to activation
•  Tracking his Stage progression to prevent Stage 4
•  Optimizing technique so he wastes less energy
•  Documenting everything so knowledge doesn’t die with them
She becomes the analyst to his ability, the pattern-reader to his intuitive reaching.
Arc Across N01-N05:
•  N01: Meets Jhace, immediately curious about the impossible thing he did
•  N02: Begins collaboration, makes first mistake, learns to trust her estimates
•  N03: Deeper partnership, makes second mistake about people vs. structures
•  N04: Intimate collaboration, makes third mistake pushing him too hard
•  N05: Ultimate trust. Everything she’s learned. Trying to keep him alive during final crisis. Fails. Her death isn’t from mistake—it’s from sacrifice. She uses Soulpulse herself (first and only time) to stabilize structure long enough for others to escape. Burns out completely. Dies knowing she saved them. Her final words to Jhace: “You’ll hold. You always do.”

[ASSISTANT]
Short version: this is already strong enough to be dangerous, but you’ve got a few landmines that will absolutely bite you later if you don’t nail them down now.

I’ll break it into:  
1) What’s working stupidly well  
2) Where it’s already contradicting your earlier canon / itself  
3) Structural tweaks to make this maximally usable as a saga bible + AI prompt-source  
4) What I’d do next
